items.objection: Why not just use Python / scikit-learn / TensorFlow?
items.underlyingConcern: Open source feels 'free'. Team already knows Python. Don't understand the gap between a Jupyter notebook and production ML in a regulated environment.

response.acknowledge: Python is excellent — your team should absolutely use Python for experimentation and model development.
response.reframe: The question isn't Python vs. SAS. The question is: what happens after the notebook? How do you move that model to production with governance, monitoring, explainability, and regulatory compliance?
response.evidence: The gap between a working Jupyter notebook and a production-governed ML system is 6-12 months of engineering: model registry, approval workflows, A/B testing, monitoring, drift detection, regulatory documentation. SAS provides all of that — and your team can still write Python inside it.
response.close: Would it be useful to see how a Python model gets promoted through SAS governance to production? It's the best of both worlds.

proofPoints: 87% of ML models never make it to production (Gartner)
Average time from notebook to production: 6-12 months without MLOps platform
SAS runs Python models with full governance — same as SAS-native models
items.whatNotToSay: Don't say 'Python isn't enterprise-grade' — it absolutely is. The gap is in production operations and governance, not in the language itself.

items.objection: Open source is free — there's no license cost
items.underlyingConcern: Budget pressure. Not differentiating between free (license) and free (total cost). Haven't considered the engineering cost of building enterprise ML infrastructure.

response.acknowledge: You're right — there's no license cost for Python libraries. That's genuinely an advantage for experimentation.
response.reframe: But 'free' has hidden costs. Who maintains the packages? Who handles security vulnerabilities? Who builds the governance, monitoring, and deployment infrastructure? Who provides support at 2am when production breaks?
response.evidence: Organizations typically spend $2-5M over 3 years building enterprise ML infrastructure from open-source components: MLflow, Kubeflow, Airflow, custom monitoring, custom governance. That's before ongoing maintenance. SAS provides this integrated — with vendor support and regulatory validation.
response.close: Let me show you a build-vs-buy comparison. The 'free' license often costs more than the commercial platform when you factor in everything needed for production.

proofPoints: Building MLOps from scratch: $2-5M over 3 years in engineering costs
Average open-source ML platform requires 3-5 FTE engineers to maintain
Security vulnerability patching in open-source packages: significant ongoing effort
items.whatNotToSay: Don't dismiss open source — respect it. Position the gap as production operations, not technology quality.

items.objection: Our university/bootcamp trained everyone on Python — nobody knows SAS
items.underlyingConcern: Hiring difficulty. Fear of skill lock-in. Perception that SAS skills are obsolete and hard to find.

response.acknowledge: You're right that more graduates learn Python first. That's the educational landscape.
response.reframe: Two things: First, SAS Viya supports Python natively — your Python-trained team works in SAS without learning a new language. Second, the learning curve for SAS-specific features (Visual Analytics, Model Studio) is days, not months — they're visual, no-code tools.
response.evidence: SAS academic program reaches 2,000+ universities globally. In the Philippines, UP, Ateneo, DLSU all have SAS coursework. And the fastest-growing SAS user base is people who write Python but use SAS for governance and deployment.
response.close: What if your Python team kept using Python but gained production governance for free? That's what SAS Viya enables — no retraining required.

proofPoints: SAS Viya runs Python natively — zero retraining for Python users
Visual tools (VA, Model Studio) have 2-3 day learning curve
SAS academic program active in 2,000+ universities including PH
items.whatNotToSay: Don't argue that SAS is 'better' than Python — that's a false dichotomy. Position SAS as the production layer for Python models.
